title: Steve Slevinski Value
subtitle: Practical DMS, repository, and research workflow value for the University of Iowa
summary: A University of Iowa-facing presentation focused on Steve Slevinski's immediate operational value through DMS planning, workflow design, code, data shaping, and repository delivery.
synopsis: Steve Slevinski is best supported first as practical research capacity. The deck focuses on immediate usefulness in the messy middle between project systems and repository delivery, showing how Steve helps projects with DMS planning, workflow design, structure, validation, and execution. The broader Sutton SignWriting infrastructure lane remains present, but only as a secondary strategic upside after the practical value is clear.

links.label: NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy
links.description: NIH guidance showing why DMS planning and execution are now core project responsibilities.

links.label: NIMH Data Archive
links.description: Repository example that illustrates the real technical burden of harmonization, validation, and recurring submission.

slides.title: Steve Slevinski Value
slides.summary: Steve brings immediate operational value through DMS planning, workflow support, data shaping, technical problem-solving, and repository delivery.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for Steve Slevinski value overview.
bullets: Practical Data Management and Sharing Plan support
Workflow design across messy project systems
slides.speech: Steve Slevinski brings immediate practical value to research teams. He can help investigators build and maintain a Data Management and Sharing Plan, work through complex data workflows, and support the path from project systems to repository delivery.
slides.notes: Open with immediate usefulness rather than the larger mission.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[DMS Plan] --> B[Workflow Design]
  B --> C[Data Shaping]
  C --> D[Repository Delivery]

slides.title: The Messy Middle
slides.summary: Many projects can collect data and name a repository target, but the difficult work sits in the messy middle where data has to be organized, transformed, documented, validated, and made deliverable.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the messy middle in research data work.
bullets: Projects need help in the middle
slides.speech: The real problem is often not collecting the data and not naming the repository. The real problem is the messy middle. Projects pull from REDCap, spreadsheets, exports, scripts, scanners, and outside systems, then have to reshape that material into something valid and deliverable. That is where Steve is useful.
slides.notes: Help the audience feel seen in their real project pain points.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Research data workflow diagram showing multiple messy local sources converging into one ordered repository submission pipeline, clean university visual
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Scattered fragments of data being pulled into a single clean structured channel

slides.title: Data Management and Sharing Plan Value
slides.summary: A DMS Plan is not just a compliance document; it is an execution problem that requires practical decisions, technical structures, and ongoing follow-through.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for DMS plan value.
bullets: Keep the plan aligned with reality over time
slides.speech: A Data Management and Sharing Plan is not only a policy document. It is a practical statement about what will actually happen to the data. Steve can help teams shape that plan, align it with real workflows, and revise it as projects change.
slides.notes: Keep this at the level of execution, not compliance theater.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[NIH Policy] --> B[DMS Plan]
  B --> C[Real Workflow]
  C --> D[Repository Delivery]

slides.title: Workflow and Code Value
slides.summary: The value becomes visible in practical outputs such as reports, scripts, transformations, validation, data structures, and repeatable workflows that make teams more effective.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for workflow and code value.
slides.speech: Steve's value is not abstract. It shows up in report design, data dictionaries, scripting, restructuring, validation, and repeatable workflows that help projects move from local complexity toward something usable and shareable.
slides.notes: Technical credibility should become unmistakable here.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Screens showing REDCap reports, CSV tables, scripts, and validation outputs in a professional research operations environment
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Clean geometric forms representing code, tables, and workflow logic locking into place

slides.title: Repository Delivery Value
slides.summary: Repository submission is where planning becomes real and the technical labor of packaging, checking, documenting, and delivering data becomes unavoidable.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for repository delivery value.
bullets: Help complete delivery, not just discuss it
slides.speech: Repository submission is often where projects discover how much work is still left. Harmonization, validation, file preparation, recurring uploads, and issue resolution all require real labor. Steve can help teams get through that work rather than stopping at advice.
slides.notes: This is the slide that makes real support feel justified.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Local Project Systems] --> B[Harmonization]
  B --> C[Validation]
  C --> D[Repository Submission]
  D --> E[Recurring Updates]

slides.title: Research Credibility
slides.summary: This value is grounded in real work around actual NIH-funded studies and active research environments rather than hypothetical consulting scenarios.
bullets: Growth and development of Striatal-Cerebellum circuitry in subjects at risk for Huntington's Disease — $11,355,231.00 — PI Peggy Nopoulos
Suicidality in Bipolar and Major Depression Disorders — $3,633,329.00 — PI Vincent Magnotta
Cerebellar metabolism, neural circuits, and symptoms in bipolar disorder — $2,302,624.00 — PI Vincent Magnotta
slides.speech: This is not theoretical value. It is grounded in work tied to real NIH-funded studies of real scale. Those examples matter because they show that the DMS and repository work sits inside projects with high institutional stakes and real delivery demands.
slides.notes: Keep the scale and seriousness visible.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: NIH-funded research portfolio slide with project titles, PI names, and grant amounts, polished and serious institutional design
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Three pillars of funded research feeding into one operational support system

slides.title: Who Benefits
slides.summary: This support is useful to one lab immediately, but the same capacity can also serve multiple labs, projects, and units across the University over time.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for who benefits from Steve's work.
slides.speech: The value is local and broad at the same time. One PI or lab can benefit directly, but the same kind of DMS and repository support can serve multiple projects across a department or across the University. That makes Steve useful not only as a project contributor, but as a cross-campus operational asset.
slides.notes: This is the institutional leverage slide.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: One technical research support role connected to multiple labs and project teams across a university network
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A central support node connected to many differently colored project pathways

slides.title: Why Salary Support Makes Sense
slides.summary: Salary support buys real capacity, availability, continuity, and technical follow-through so useful work can happen when projects actually need it.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for salary support case.
bullets: A stronger base for future grants
slides.speech: Salary support buys real capacity. It increases availability, supports deeper technical engagement, improves continuity for active projects, and creates a stronger institutional base for future grant activity.
slides.notes: Keep this practical, direct, and free of rescue framing.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Salary Support] --> B[Availability]
  A --> C[Execution Capacity]
  A --> D[Continuity]
  A --> E[Grant Readiness]

slides.title: Immediate Value and Strategic Value
slides.summary: The immediate pitch is practical support for current research work, while the strategic upside is broader infrastructure growth that can emerge from that base.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for immediate and strategic value.
bullets: Secondary upside: stronger footing for Sutton SignWriting
slides.speech: Start with practical support for active projects. The strategic upside is that this also creates a stronger base for grant development, future collaboration, and, secondarily, a more stable footing for Sutton SignWriting as shared research infrastructure. That larger benefit is real, but it works best as an added upside, not the first reason to say yes.
slides.notes: The broader mission belongs here: late, secondary, and credible.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Two-tiered strategy slide with immediate project support on the first level and longer-term infrastructure opportunity above it
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A sturdy lower platform supporting a higher emerging structure

slides.title: Closing Ask
slides.summary: The closing ask is to use Steve for practical DMS and repository work now and to support deeper engagement where that capacity will matter most.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the closing ask.
bullets: Use Steve as a practical University resource
Include salary support where deeper work is needed
Let immediate utility create long-term opportunity
slides.speech: The closing ask is straightforward. Use Steve as a practical University resource for DMS planning, workflow design, and repository delivery. Where deeper involvement is needed, include salary support. That support creates immediate project value and also leaves open a longer-term opportunity for stronger collaboration and infrastructure growth.
slides.notes: End with usefulness first and future second.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Use Steve Now] --> B[Better Project Delivery]
  B --> C[Salary Support Where Needed]
  C --> D[Long-Term Opportunity]
